vocabulary.definition: a penalty for something you did wrong
vocabulary.example: The escape of the Brazilian boa constrictor earned Harry his longest ever punishment.

vocabulary.definition: to walk around without a fixed route or purpose
vocabulary.example: This was why Harry spent as much time as possible out of the house, wandering around and thinking about the end of the holidays, where he could see a tiny ray of hope.

vocabulary.definition: special clothes worn for school or work
thick paper made from animal skin, used for important documents
vocabulary.example: The envelope was thick and heavy, made of yellowish parchment, and the address was written in emerald green ink.

vocabulary.definition: a paper cover for a letter
vocabulary.example: He handed Uncle Vernon the bill and the postcard, sat down, and slowly began to open the yellow envelope.

vocabulary.definition: to shake because you are nervous or excited
vocabulary.example: Turning the envelope over, his hand trembling, Harry saw a purple wax seal bearing a coat of arms; a lion, an eagle, a badger, and a snake surrounding a large letter H.

vocabulary.definition: to breathe in suddenly, often from shock

vocabulary.example: “I want to read it,” said Harry furiously, “as it’s mine.”

vocabulary.definition: to fight by grabbing and trying to pin someone down
vocabulary.example: Uncle Vernon had to wrestle Dudley to the ground to get the letter from him, which was made difficult by the fact that Harry had grabbed Uncle Vernon around the neck from behind.

vocabulary.definition: to cover openings with wooden planks
vocabulary.example: After burning all the letters, he got out a hammer and nails and boarded up the cracks around the front and back doors so no one could go out.

vocabulary.definition: the pipe above a fireplace where smoke goes out
vocabulary.example: Something came whizzing down the kitchen chimney as he spoke and caught him sharply on the back of the head.
Perched on top of the rock was the most miserable little shack you could imagine.

characters.name: Harry Potter
characters.description: A boy about to turn eleven who has never received mail before.

characters.name: Uncle Vernon
characters.description: Panics when letters arrive for Harry and tries every trick to stop them.

characters.name: Dudley Dursley
characters.description: Starts at Smeltings and fights Harry for the mysterious letters.

background.hook: After the zoo incident, Harry endures a long punishment. Then strange letters begin arriving for him — addressed down to the cupboard under the stairs — and the Dursleys will do anything to keep him from reading them.
background.readingFocus: Watch how the Dursleys react each time a new letter appears. Notice how the address on the letters changes as Harry moves rooms.

options.text: a penalty for doing something wrong
a paper cover for a letter

miniCheck.stem: If someone is furious, they are —

comprehension.stem: What is this chapter mainly about?

options.text: Mysterious letters keep arriving for Harry, and the Dursleys try desperately to stop him from reading them
Dudley wins a prize at Smeltings
Harry learns to fly a broomstick

comprehension.explanation: The chapter follows the growing flood of letters and the Dursleys' increasingly extreme attempts to hide them from Harry.

comprehension.stem: How is Harry's first letter addressed?

options.text: Mr. H. Potter, The Cupboard under the Stairs, 4 Privet Drive
Mr. Potter, The Attic, Privet Drive

comprehension.explanation: The first letter is addressed plainly to Harry in the cupboard under the stairs.

comprehension.stem: What is unusual about the first letter's envelope?

options.text: It is thick yellowish parchment with green ink and no stamp
It is bright pink with a Hogwarts logo
It is emailed to the Dursleys
It arrives by owl at breakfast

comprehension.explanation: The envelope is heavy parchment, the address is in emerald green ink, and there is no stamp.

comprehension.stem: How does Harry feel when he picks up the first letter?

options.text: Shocked and thrilled — no one has ever written to him before

comprehension.explanation: Harry stares at the letter, his heart twanging — no one in his whole life had ever written to him.

comprehension.stem: What does Uncle Vernon do to the mail slot?

options.text: He gives Harry the only key

comprehension.explanation: Uncle Vernon stays home and nails up the mail slot so letters cannot be delivered.
comprehension.pageHint: after Harry tries to catch the post

comprehension.stem: Why does Uncle Vernon let Harry move into Dudley's second bedroom?

options.text: He hopes to bribe Harry after burning the first letter, though Harry cares more about the letter than the room
Dudley asks him to share nicely

comprehension.explanation: Vernon burns the letter and then awkwardly offers the bedroom, but Harry says the letter had his cupboard on it and was no mistake.

comprehension.stem: How do letters get into the house on Sunday morning?

options.text: They shoot down the kitchen chimney and out of the fireplace
They arrive by owl through the window
They are hidden inside Dudley's tortoise

comprehension.explanation: Something whizzes down the chimney and dozens of letters come pelting out of the fireplace.

vocabulary.explanation: Harry earns his longest ever punishment after the boa constrictor escapes.

vocabulary.stem: The envelope was thick and heavy, made of yellowish ___, and the address was written in emerald green ink.
vocabulary.explanation: Harry's first letter arrives on unusual parchment with a purple wax seal.
Harry's hand trembles as he turns the envelope over to look at the seal.

vocabulary.stem: Which word is closest in meaning to 'miserable'?
vocabulary.explanation: The shack on the rock is described as the most miserable little shack you could imagine.

vocabulary.stem: Something came whizzing down the kitchen ___ as he spoke and caught him sharply on the back of the head.
vocabulary.explanation: On Sunday, letters pour down the chimney when Vernon claims there is no post.

cloze.title: The Letters from No One
paragraphs: After the zoo, Harry faced his longest ever (1) ___. When the first letter arrived, it was written on yellowish (2) ___ inside a heavy (3) ___ with green ink.
Uncle Vernon (4) ___ when he read it and burned the mail. He later (5) ___ up the doors and nailed shut the mail slot, but letters still came down the (6) ___.
The Dursleys fled to a (7) ___ little shack on a rock, where Harry waited for his eleventh birthday and heard a knock at the door.

gaps.explanation: The boa constrictor escape earned Harry his longest punishment.
The first letter is on thick yellowish parchment.
Harry slowly opens the yellow envelope at breakfast.
Uncle Vernon gasps Petunia's name after glancing at the letter.
Vernon boards up the cracks around the doors.
Letters whiz down the kitchen chimney on Sunday.
They hide in the most miserable little shack on a rock.
